============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Brad Sheneman
Date: 2 Jul 2015

Hello all,

I am working on representing world data from the Minecraft server in 

OpenCog's 3DSpaceMap, but I have a few questions. First, here is a 

breakdown of the situation:



The server sends packets containing 'chunks' of world data depending on the 

 [...]

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-03

It's not sparse, which is why I suggest replacing the Octree inside

the 3DSpaceMap with an OctoMap (which could also be wrapped inside the

3DSpaceMap)





b) What the bot is aware of, I guess



Though there should also be information about what the bot can see at

each current time...



-- ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Minecraft Embodiment (14c27bdba637a9ab)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Minecraft Embodiment
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-03-17 to 2015-03-24
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c27bdba637a9ab

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Morgan Creekmore
Date: 2015-03-17

Greetings, I have noticed quite a few posts about Minecraft embodiment with OpenCog and using spock. I have been working with nickelpro to update spock to be a more usable interface. I was wondering what features OpenCog would like to be added.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-03-18

Hi Morgan, That's great. Can you describe the changes made recently? It would also be interesting to see what modifications the micropsi2 project has needed to make, which is also using Spock.

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Morgan Creekmore
Date: 2015-03-24

Things that have been added semi recently: basic collision and physics (jumping, bounding box collision, gravity), world plugin speedups, inventory management (chests, furnaces), and a pull request is open to add crafting logic.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Minecraft Pathfinding with the URE (14f9adc63d262a33)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Minecraft Pathfinding with the URE
Messages: 2
Date range: 4 Sep 2015 to 2015-09-05
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f9adc63d262a33

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Fredy
Date: 4 Sep 2015

Hi All, Can someone point me to the URE code and any updated documentation we have? My goal is to use URE to implement pathfinding for the Minecraft embodiment code.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-09-05

Hola Bohemio,

The URE has been under active development and the current wiki and markdown documentation is not great...

About pathfinding and the URE: For general background, a Prolog implementation of A* navigation is described at https://www.cpp.edu/~jrfisher/www/prolog_tutorial/5_1.html

The best way to proceed is to understand the ocPlanner theory and port ocPlanner to the URE. The beauty is that one gets not only pure pathfinding, but pathfinding with 'building and moving blocks as needed to make or clear a path'.

-- ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+ML Problem (14f83e8fed262be5)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: ML Problem
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-08-31 to 2015-08-31
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f83e8fed262be5

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Kakarla Dinesh
Date: 2015-08-31

Can anyone suggest me an algorithm that takes input classes and continuous input and outputs multiclass?

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-09-01

OpenCog MOSES can do this, so can SVM or decision forests. In their standard forms all of these would output binary category labels, not n-ary. A lot of history shows that learning an ensemble of one-versus-all classifiers tends to do better.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-31

The continuous-input side of MOSES should work well if/when all of the particle-swarm code gets fully integrated; but I do not think that it is well-tested yet. My experience is that continuous-input data tends to be very noisy, and things just work better if you quantize it into bins and treat it as discrete.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-08-31

Linas, I do not understand what you mean by diagonalize it first using SVM, could you be more explicit?

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-31

Maybe I should just say orthogonalize instead of SVM. If I have 20 continuous-valued input features, many are likely strongly correlated. Binning them is likely to erase these correlations. Better to diagonalize the feature-set into orthogonal components first. SVM or PCA or perceptrons, it is all the same thing: kernel machines.

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-31

I just added this to the wiki idea page: Use kernel methods to pre-filter continuous data.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+MOSES with more than one Y-variable (151d1ea1eb985325)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: MOSES with more than one Y-variable
Messages: 3
Date range: 23 Dec 2015 to 29 Dec 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151d1ea1eb985325

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: anticancer8108@gmail.com
Date: 23 Dec 2015

Hi everyone, I have been using moses for awhile. Is there a way to get more than one root node in a combo tree? I know I can make more than one combo tree for each Y variable but can I do it with a single combo tree?

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-12-28

The C++ tree container allows to have multiple root nodes. However MOSES does not support that. So my advice is to run separate learning instances instead.

Nil

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: anticancer8108@gmail.com
Date: 29 Dec 2015

Great thanks for the answer! but did you mean MOSES does NOT support that?

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Multimodal Recurrent Neural Architecture generates sentence descriptions from... (14ef2b255d58572a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Multimodal Recurrent Neural Architecture generates sentence descriptions from...
Messages: 7
Date range: 2015-08-03 to 2015-08-08
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ef2b255d58572a

============================================================

--- Message 1/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-03

Available code from Karpathy neuraltalk. I think we could improve this via utilizing the output of our OpenCog NLP system as additional input features.

--- Message 2/7 ---
From: Yuhuang Hu
Date: 2015-08-03

Neural talk is quite popular but complex to study. You can search show, attend and tell for a more structured way plus an attention model.

--- Message 3/7 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-04

We could turn it around, and use those sentence descriptions as input to opencog. It would be as if opencog was blind, but this companion on the side describes what it sees. A way of getting pre-processed visual data into opencog.

--- Message 4/7 ---
From: Dagmawi Moges
Date: 2015-08-07

I love to work on this...

--- Message 5/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-07

Cool, lets do it!

--- Message 6/7 ---
From: Tejas Khot
Date: 2015-08-07

I would like to work on this too!

--- Message 7/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-08

From a near-term product-dev point of view, as Linas has suggested, it could be interesting to simply connect this existing system to OpenCog and to a Hanson robot. From an AGI perspective, the challenge is to pursue further work on joint linguistic/visual learning. While very cool, this work does not produce any internal representation that could be used for reasoning. One class of approach would be to parse sentences using the OpenCog NLP pipeline, and then learn mappings between vectorial representations of images and subtrees of sentence interpretation-structure.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Negation weirdness (150a7a1a27bd02bd)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Negation weirdness
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-10-27 to 2015-10-27
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150a7a1a27bd02bd

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-10-27

Ruiting and I discussed this last night. She was wondering if an approach conceptually related to QuantitativePredicate might help. What if we created a NormalizedTruthValue which basically consists of a quantile-normalized version of a PredicateNode's truth value? Then negation would act the same way on every PredicateNode's NormalizedTruthValue.

-- Ben

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Misgana Bayetta
Date: 2015-10-27

The python implementation is here https://github.com/misgeatgit/opencog/tree/quantitative-predicates/opencog/python/quantitativePredicate. We were waiting for Ramin's more general test data when we paused.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Neo4j Backing Store for OpenCog rough design sketch (14a427d96ed0fc25)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Neo4j Backing Store for OpenCog rough design sketch
Messages: 54
Date range: 2014-12-13 to 2015-01-10
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14a427d96ed0fc25

============================================================

--- Message 1/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-13

Hi all,



There has been lots of talk over the years about extending OpenCog's

current backing store functionality



Here is a concrete proposal for doing so



http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Neo4j_Backing_Store



The proposal is to use Neo4j in a particular way.  I note that we now

have a bit of experience with Neo4j as Rodas and Eskender have used it

for a commercial project.  Also, I have been in contact during the

last year with  a large company using Neo4j for a proprietary

OpenCoggish proto-AGI project (and somewhat interested in OpenCog as

well)....



The crux of my proposal is a particular way of mapping a certain class

of OpenCog pattern-matching queries (not extremely general, but I

think general enough to be widely useful) into queries that can be

efficiently executed...

--- Message 2/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-13

Hi Ben,



Finally! :D



anyways, using REST API might make things a little sluggish. There is a

project in github called LightSocket

<https://github.com/Squelsh/lightsocket>that seems to be the closest thing

you can get for C++ bindings. You might as well check it out.

--- Message 3/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-14

Thanks, maybe LightSocket will be a good idea.  For pattern-matchy

queries, the overhead of the REST API won't matter, but for quick

queries it might...

--- Message 4/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-14

Actually LightSocket looks not to be an active project; is there some

more recent analogue?



ben

--- Message 5/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-13

Not totally sure if there are any other projects with C++ in mind (Neo4j

supports a wide range of programming languages, so not much incentive I

think). But I guess we can always implement a python module for CogServer

and use the Neo4j python bindings.

--- Message 6/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-13

A python module will go better with the current approach of not being

dependent on the backing store too IMO.

--- Message 7/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-13

Well, OpenCog is also distributed, and has been for about 5 years, but no

one seems to ever be interested in using it that way.   It works now, no

extnsions or changes are needed.



-- Linas

--- Message 8/54 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2014-12-14

Hi,



To help think about this in a concrete way, how would the following atom be

expressed in the Cypher query language?



EvaluationLink

  PredicateNode "eats"

  ListLink

      ConceptNode "Robot_1"

      ConceptNode "Batteries"



Thanks,

Cosmo

--- Message 9/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-15

(node1 {name:"Robot_1"})-[:EATS]→(node2 {name:"Batteries"})



... I think...



To find everything that eats batteries:



(node1)-[:EATS]→(node2 {name:"Batteries"})



To find everything labeled Person that eats batteries:



(node1:Person)-[:EATS]→(node2 {name:"Batteries"})



Extrapolating from



http://neo4j.com/developer/cypher-query-language/#_relationships



On Mon, Dec 15, 2014 at 2:24 PM, Cosmo Harrigan

<cosmo.harrigan@singularityu.org> wrote:

--- Message 10/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-15

I don't expect it to be hard to translate EvaluationLinks into neo4j

graphs.  Where I do expect problems are in finding all incoming links

(which, as a corollary, has that all atoms are unique).  So: find

everything that has PredicateNode "eats" embedded in it.  Or find

everything that has ConceptNode "Robot_1" in it.



Under the covers, this is more-or-less all that the pattern matcher

requires: the ability to find the incoming set  (as presumably, finding the

outgoing set is easy).  Give me that ability, the rest is 'easy'.



Again: the backingstore API is really very simple. Again: you can get a

working distributed atomspace today, if you so wish it.   Its not clear to

me why neo4j would be better -- mostly it seems just different.



-- Linas



On Mon, Dec 15, 2014 at 12:24. [...]

--- Message 11/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-16

Finding the incoming set of an Atom is done "automagically" via Neo4j

when you submit an appropriate Cypher query.   Of course this entails

a lot of work under the surface of the Cypher query language.   But,

they have put a bunch of energy into optimizing precisely this sort of

query, because it's the kind of query one generally wants to ask of a

graph database...



-- Ben

--- Message 12/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-16

IMO if there is a threat in using Neo4j, it would not be anything "graph"

related, but queries that have to do with "hyper-graph" side of things.

Although I'm not familiar with all kinds of Atomspace's queries, I guess a

query involving a hyper-link might make things a little complex. The...

--- Message 13/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-16

All hypergraphs are just a special kind of graph, called a "Levi graph".

See wikipedia. If something can handle ordinary graphs, then it can handle

hypergraphs. There's no magic here.  This is exactly how the atomspace

works, BTW.



All databases have indexes in them that are designed so that certain

queries can be pre-processed and retrieved very quickly. The atomspace is

no exception.



All major database engines allow the user to create user-defined indexes.

I assume neo4j is no exception.  A good dbadmin knows how to design indexes

so that fast performance results.  Its an important part of that job.



There has long been a proposal to add user-defined indexes to the

atomspace. It would not be hard. But no one has asked for this, no one has

expressed any interest.



There. [...]

--- Message 14/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-17

well a simple example that comes to mind for a query that can't be done in

tractable time with the current approach is when the sub-graph that is to

be investigated can't fit in the memory. Then you should run the query on

the bakingstore itself. A simple query like "friends of friends of...

--- Message 15/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-16

Clearly, if you wish to distort reality, that is always possible. Just

because something is written down on some web page somewhere does not mean

that its true or correct.  Al I get out of that particular demo is that

who-ever wrote it is ignorant of what databases are, or how they work.



I guess there is also a different conclusion: if one is ignorant of

databases and how to use them, then perhaps, for certain types of queries,

neo4j has an easier-to-use API.



--linas

--- Message 16/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-17

Well, that ignorant dude has data and an expriment which can be recreated

(even provided the code). You can always "undistort" the reality for

everyone with your own. Till then let's stick to the facts.



On Wed, Dec 17, 2014 at 1:47 AM, Linas Vepstas <linasvepstas@gmail.com>

wrote:

--- Message 17/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-17

This one <http://neo4j.com/news/relational-database-graph-database/> may be

more to your taste. It's from the Neo4j team so I cannot roll out that it

is biased.

--- Message 18/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-16

Ramin, its pointless to fight with you, but honestly, that page is just a

boatload of crap.   I'm sorry if you can't see that. I don't have time to

argue about crap.



--linas

--- Message 19/54 ---
From: Matt Chapman
Date: 2014-12-16

Hi Linas,



Do you have time to explain what would be the current approach to the case

"when the sub-graph that is to be investigated can't fit in the memory" ?

E.g., assuming the current Postgres storage backend?



All the Best,



Matt



On Tue, Dec 16, 2014 at 2:45 PM, Linas Vepstas...

--- Message 20/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-16

Can't do that, until you provide a scenario where that is actually a

problem.



I'm saying something very simple: don't solve problems that don't exist. We

have a lot of real issues; trying to solve imaginary ones doesn't make

sense.



Right now, you can buy 32GB of ram for about $300 and motherboards that can

take 256GB for about $300 as well. -- this will hold gazallions of atoms.

At the current performance of the atomspace, you could only touch each atom

once every few days or week, per cpu core.  With the current PLN design, I

don't believe its possible to run out of RAM, and if you do, there are

certainly some very easy solutions.



--linas

--- Message 21/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-17

Does anyone have an OK estimate of how much RAM is occupied by the set

of Atoms produced via parsing a (say ten-word) sentence using the link

parser, RelEx and RelEx2Logic, and storing all the outputs in the

Atomspace?



This would be useful to know in terms of thinking about hardware

requirements for NLP work.



The kind of near-term use-case that would benefit from a more flexibly

query-able backing store (which could, I recognize, be implemented in

many different ways) would be one in which it was necessary to match

queries against a large body of data.   In this case, the average

piece of data in "long term memory" would be visited very

infrequently, but it is important to have a lot of data on hand for

answering queries that may come up.



NL question answering against a. [...]

--- Message 22/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-17

Well there are roughly 10M articles in Wikipedia, if every article

contribute only "one" atom to the Atomspace and every atom takes "a byte"

of memory, you would need 9.5GB of RAM to store only the Wikipedia. Make

that "three" atoms or "three" bytes and your out of your $300 worth...

--- Message 23/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-17

OpenCog's NL comprehension pipeline could be used now for information

extraction from Wikipedia.  However we plan to start by playing with

Simple English Wikipedia, as it's smaller and the sentences are less

contorted...



ben

--- Message 24/54 ---
From: Ramin Barati
Date: 2014-12-17

Well, Simple English Wikipedia has 118641 articles according to itself

<http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Size_of_Wikipedia>, I did a not so

reliable count and every article has 2746.5 word on average. That means

there are roughly 32.6M sentences of 10 words in Simple English...

--- Message 25/54 ---
From: Chris N
Date: 2014-12-16

Not to get too far into the argument here, but thought I'd contribute a

little since I currently do both NLP and Graph-based relationship

processing on Full English Wikipedia (~5M docs), I thought I'd pipe in

quickly. We currently structure both a Lucene/Solr index for the text...

--- Message 26/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-17

Thanks Ramin



So to fit SEW in 32 GB of RAM we'd need something like 100 bytes per word....



In 2010, some basic measurement from the Atomspace were



***

Atom = 64 bytes

Node = 72 bytes

Link = 96 bytes



Indexes: roughly a total of 400-500 bytes an atom

***



So if we



--  take 500 bytes per Atom as an order of magnitude estimate for the

total, including indices



-- take an estimate of 300 million word-instances in SEW



-- Assume that the average word has 2.5 link parser links, 2.5 RelEx

relation links, and 2.5 RelEx2Logic links ... then consider extra

links for words versus word-instances versus concepts etc. ... we

probably get something like 5-10 links per word even accounting for

some links joining multiple words.  Let's call it 10 just for

simplicity.



Then.. [...]

--- Message 27/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-17

As for advantages of Postgres vs. Neo4j vs. HGDB vs. MongoDB and many

other alternatives, obviously there are many considerations....



Taste and ease of use given peoples' backgrounds is one kind of

consideration -- personally, I kinda like Neo4j and HGDB, and I

dislike configuring relational databases....  But I totally admit I am

not an expert on RDBs ...



Performance is a subtle point, because all of these persistence

solutions can be configured in so many different ways.   For instance:

Frequently graph DBs are measured to be better than RDBs at various

sorts of graph-oriented queries ... but would they be better than an

RDB really well configured for that particular kind of task?   I don't

know....   One can then talk about how efficient a certain persistence

solution...

--- Message 28/54 ---
From: David Hart
Date: 2014-12-17

1.5 TB is not terribly a lot of RAM for 2014.



3 TB & 6 TB configurations are available in near-commodity-priced

off-the-shelf servers from all of the major vendors; here's Dell's machine:

http://www.dell.com/us/business/p/poweredge-r920/pd



I should think that trying to perform operations...

--- Message 29/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-17

Agreed, interactive processing with Atomspace fragments on disk is not

going to be feasible.



However, keeping huge Atomspaces on disk, and then selectively

retrieving portions of them into RAM based on relevance, is certainly

feasible...



And as we all know, persistence solutions like Neo4j or Postgres or

whatever can move data back and forth from RAM to disk judiciously

within their own scope, according to their own hopefully

useful/adaptive methods...



As for





that's true for commercial projects, but it's not true from the

standpoint of the average OpenCog developer doing OpenCog work on

their own machine...





-- Ben

--- Message 30/54 ---
From: David Hart
Date: 2014-12-18

Developers doing work on varied and weak local machines makes little sense

for the upcoming stages of the dialog project, particularly when no

technical hurdles exist for sharing a single powerful development machine;

tools like mosh and docker make this sharing a breeze, with the added...

--- Message 31/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-17

I've gotten bitten by the docker bug... it now seems to me that if/when we

finally have a collection of data we can share, we should do so in a Docker

container.  I have not yet personally tried this, but this seems to be one

of the use-cases for docker.



I like this, because I've previously been concerned that you'd have to be a

whiz-band DB admin in order to share data. Which docker, that does not have

to be the case.



--linas

--- Message 32/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-17

Ramin,



You are welcome to implement all kinds of designs and features. I'm pushing

back because we have limited resources and time, and don't want to spend

that effort foolishly.



One problem is that, until we have a functioning and usable PLN, and have

some experience with it, and also have a workable attention allocation

system, we really don't know what the access patterns to the atomspace will

be like. This makes any sort of tuning or optimization risky or impossible.



To be precise: any claim that somehow neo4j will make things better is not

anchored in objective reality, simply because we do not know what that

reality is .. and we won't, until we have a working PLN+AA and have built

up experience with it.



Five years ago, the hip things to talk about were Hypertable. [...]

--- Message 33/54 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2014-12-18

yes it would be possible by using docker volumes. I will be dockerizing

postgres for surface realization tasks so we will know more soon



On Thu Dec 18 2014 at 9:22:16 AM Linas Vepstas <linasvepstas@gmail.com>

wrote:

--- Message 34/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-17

Chris N had a very good answer to all of this.  You don't have to fit allof

wikipedia in RAM. We have a functioning postgres backend, now. It can store

petabytes.  If you need more than petabtes, there's the dusty broken

hypertable code.  There are other possibilities, as chrisN points out.



--linas

--- Message 35/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-17

I have huge concerns that we will not be able to do interactive processing

at all, on anything larger than a gig or two: I fully expect CPU to be the

bottleneck, not RAM.  We are not a big-data outfit, doing lightweight

processing on large data.  We are a cpu-intensive processor of medium-small

data.  Which is yet another reason this conversation makes me squirm.







Docker!

--- Message 36/54 ---
From: David Hart
Date: 2014-12-18

On Thu, Dec 18, 2014 at 9:55 AM, Linas Vepstas <linasvepstas@gmail.com>

wrote:



One thing we have previously not discussed is a cluster architecture for

OpenCog with a simple star topology, one with a BIG-RAM machine at the

central hub and CPU-intensive machines at the leaf nodes, utilizing...

--- Message 37/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-17

well, once again, I really really dislike the idea of designing a cluster

arhcitecture of any sort, simply because we don't know where the

bottlenecks really are. we don't know how the data will move about, so we

simply don't know where the zmq connections should be. should they be

inside the atomsapce? perhaps inside the patterns matcher? Or maybe inside

of pln? or maybe somewhere else, in attention allocation? I can't begin to

guess, and I've been doing this for a while. you have to build these

systems, and actually find out how they work, before you can start

optimizing them for performance.



It feels like we are arguing about how to best tune and optimize a warp

drive, when we haven't built a working warp drive yet ...



--linas

--- Message 38/54 ---
From: David Hart
Date: 2014-12-18

Placing a BIG-RAM machine at the center of a cluster is not about designing

a new cluster architecture, but rather taking one that already works (the

PostgreSQL storage model) and enhancing it in such as way as it is

practically guaranteed to perform better (because atoms would be stored

in-RAM. [...]

--- Message 40/54 ---

Hi Chris,



I totally agree with you in regards with using the tool that works best in

--- Message 42/54 ---
From: Chris Nolan
Date: 2014-12-19

Hi Ramin,



I'm not sure any of us have a good solution yet :-) and I think the problem is solutions for one domain often don't generalize well. But, as a common rule I've found that a hybrid approach seems to offer the best flexibility, and then resources can be individually allocated per service. [...]

--- Message 43/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-20

BTW, Neo4j internally uses Lucene for inverted indexing.



When you manually create an index within Neo4j, it uses Lucene to do

this, and then the index can be directly accessed using Lucene syntax



Therefore, if using Neo4j, there is no need to separately set up

inverted indexing using Lucene or other tools.



Further, in Neo4j, one can store large strings like text documents as

node properties, which will then only be loaded into RAM upon request.

  Many people have done this w/ text documents like web pages or blog

posts, without encountering performance problems.



All in alll, it is not clear to me that the complexity of using

multiple backing stores together is warranted...



-- Ben G

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/25532029/neo4j-setting-up-full-text-index-and-fuzzy-search-cypher-query



though it's not as elegant to set up as one might like (they plan to

address this in a later version)



I agree that a tool with a more free OSS license would be nice, and

HGDB seems to supply that (it's LGPL).



My suggestion was to extend the BackingStore API into a

GraphBackingStore API, which would allow experimentation with Neo4j

and HGDB (and potentially others) side by side, to see how the

for...

--- Message 47/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-22

Neo4j is pretty slick by now.   I like it.   Definitely recommended

for those who want to get used to graph DB ish thinking



Bear in mind of course they've had a lot of venture $$ funding their

development, so they've had the ability to hire lots of good

developers just to add nice bells and whistles to their original

somewhat crude graphDB.   But unlike most VC-funded firms they have

put the $ to good use ;)



HGDB may be equally or more capable ---- I haven't tried them side by

side.   HGDB has a more flexible representation internally

(hypergraphs!), but I'm unsure if it's as performant as Neo4j.  But

HGDB has been a volunteer-based OSS project so it hasn't had the $$ to

put in the kinds of lovely features that Neo4j now has...



Thinking in semantic graphs is kinda step 1. [...]

From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2014-12-29

Hi,



Okay, I tried constructing a few simple examples in Cypher, which are

available here:

https://github.com/cosmoharrigan/opencog-neo4j



To start with, consider a simple binary relation, "Robot_1 eats Batteries."



(EvaluationLink

  (PredicateNode "eats")

  (ListLink

   (ConceptNode "Robot_1")

   (ConceptNode "Batteries")))



which we could write in Cypher as



CREATE

  (n1:Concept {name: "Robot_1"}),

  (n2:Concept {name: "Batteries"}),

  (n1)-[:EATS]->(n2);



and then we could ask, "What eats batteries?" as



MATCH

  (n:Concept)-[:EATS]->(b:Concept {name: "Batteries"})

RETURN

  (n);



But what if we want to express an n-ary relation? Consider the n-ary

relation, "Zeno gave Einstein a battery." Currently we can express this in

the AtomSpace utilizing the feature.. [...]

--- Message 51/54 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-29

Bother of these examples seem to be built on flawed logic: the presumption

that it is somehow more efficient to do the query in a different address

space -- viz, doing the query in the address space of the database, instead

of the atomspace.  But really, what is the difference?  Why would it be

more efficient to do it there, instead of here?



If you can answer that question, then there is another question: what

prevents us from having the same efficiencies in the atomspace?



--linas

--- Message 52/54 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2014-12-30

Hmmm...



I'm not saying it will be more efficient to submit Pattern Match type

queries against an in-RAM Neo4j or HGDB database, than against the

AtomSpace ...



The issue at hand is persistent storage, right?   One would like to be

able to submit a wider variety of queries against an "Atomspace on

disk", rather than only being able to look up individual Atoms by

name/Handle.   So this requires one to deal with indexing Atoms that

are stored on disk in ways that are similar to (many of) those now

used for Atoms in RAM....   It also seems to benefit from well-tuned

caching for frequently accessed Atoms on disk, etc.



There is nothing here that strictly requires some fancy third-party

tool, of course.   It could all be done directly using, say,

BerkeleyDB (which is basically.. [...]

From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-01-10

Thanks,

Cosmo

terminology), and it seems the natural approach...

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+New Atomspace Repository (14d04d509d02191c)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: New Atomspace Repository
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-04-29 to 2015-04-29
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14d04d509d02191c

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-04-29

Curtis, Everyone,

I've split out the atomspace-"only" part of opencog to a new directory: it is at github.com/opencog/atomspace -- it contains everything that isn't tied to a cogserver or an agent. Of the 77 unit tests, all but two pass: two of the 6 or 7 python tests. Disentangling python was the hardest, since there were blurred lines between cogserver init and python init.

The next step for me is to carve out the atomspace parts from opencog.

--linas

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-04-29

I understand the work-around, but its not applicable in this case. The ONLY difference is that I have two different versions of atomspace.so. I copied one from a non-working build, and one from a working build. So, without recompiling anything, I can simply copy the good atomspace.so in place, and everything works.

Thus, I conclude its not really atomspace.so that is busted; its some other library that atomspace.so is linking to. Without an error message from Pyx_ImportModule, I can't figure out what to chase.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-04-29

Just to be clear, this traps the crash:

import_opencog__atomspace() sets the py_atomspace() function pointer if the cython module load succeeded. But the function pointer will be NULL if the opencog.atomspace cython module failed to load. Avert a hard-to-debug crash on null-pointer-deref.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+New developer help (151e356b38ff3b7a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: New developer help
Messages: 3
Date range: 27 Dec 2015 to 27 Dec 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151e356b38ff3b7a

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Sagar Kar
Date: 27 Dec 2015

Hi, I am new to opensource contribution. I know C/C++ and little of python. Do I have something to contribute in OpenCog?

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-27

Hi Sagar, Welcome! A few questions to grok your background: How is your knowledge of data structures and algorithms? Are you familiar with predicate logic, forward and backward chaining? Evolutionary learning? Clustering?

ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Karan Desai
Date: 27 Dec 2015

Hi Ben, I am also new to Open Source. I am comfortable with C++, JAVA as well as Python. I set up the Opencog development environment today. I am familiar with Predicate Logic, Data Structures, and have used K-Means clustering through scikit-learn.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+New Developer please help ) (151f4546cdcad98e)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: New Developer please help )
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-12-30 to 2015-12-31
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151f4546cdcad98e

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: ankit kumar
Date: 2015-12-30

I am Ankit Kumar from IIT Guwahati, interested in contributing to OpenCog. I have taken Machine Learning and Data Mining courses. I have experience with clustering algorithms. How should I get started if I wish to contribute in C++?

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: ankit kumar
Date: 2015-12-30

It appears there is clustering related work. Can somebody help me out with the starting point for it?

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-12-30

The thing to do is to review the wiki Ideas page and see if one of these tickles your fancy, and might be something you could make progress with.

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-31

My idea is to implement agglomerative clustering using the URE (Unified Rule Engine). To work in this direction, you should learn to use the Pattern Matcher and the URE. I would suggest to do this in a mix of Scheme and C++; you should become familiar with the OpenCog Scheme shell.

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: ankit kumar
Date: 2015-12-31

Thanks! I would like to work on this idea. I would do this in Scheme and C++. I am going through the tutorials and setting up the Scheme shell.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+new idea (14c2e19cb304c667)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: new idea
Messages: 2
Date range: 18 Mar 2015 to 2015-03-18
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c2e19cb304c667

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: prakhardogra@gmail.com
Date: 18 Mar 2015

Hello mentors, I am 3rd year undergraduate student at Delhi Technological University. I have a background in Machine Learning. I would really appreciate if you allow new ideas for projects.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-18

Possibly ... but not likely. Any proposal would have to fit together and work with the pieces that we currently have, and be designed in a way so that it would be useful for future work. It would need to extend the existing system.

Perhaps you could say more?

-- Linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+New simplified ECAN equations (14bde318d9ff8cd8)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: New simplified ECAN equations
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-03-03 to 2015-03-03
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14bde318d9ff8cd8

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: matt.ikle@gmail.com
Date: 2015-03-03

Attached is a document summarizing proposed changes to the ECAN equations. Changes include: 1) elimination of explicit InverseHebbianLinks, though functionality is preserved; 2) simplified normalization procedures.

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-03-02

Hi Matt, It's nice to see these updates. I have two questions: 1. Are negative STI values allowed? 2. What if there are atoms with s_i < tax or s_i = 0? Then their STI could decrease without bound. Is that desirable?

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-03

Matt, Perhaps the rent update equation needs modification?

Ben

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: matt.ikle@gmail.com
Date: 2015-03-03

Good questions — I pretty much left the homeostatic bounds equations untouched from what is in the current code. For the purposes of the dynamics I was thinking of the STI values as all nonnegative. We could perhaps impose some minimal nonnegative STI value.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+New to Open Source (14ef703affb8f0c4)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: New to Open Source
Messages: 3
Date range: 3 Aug 2015 to 4 Aug 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ef703affb8f0c4

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: himanshu sharma
Date: 3 Aug 2015

Hi everyone, I am new to Open Source and I want to contribute to OpenCog. I know java and have a very keen interest in Artificial intelligence and machine learning.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-04

Hi!

There are many ways to participate. One way is to select one of the projects listed on the ideas page: http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Ideas although its probably even better for beginners to go through the list of open bug reports and try fixing some of them.

By fixing bugs, you get to learn the code base, and understand how things fit together.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: himanshu sharma
Date: 4 Aug 2015

Thank You sir

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+no argv for variable number of arguments in GroundedSchemaNode Python functions (1504dab5dec87a9a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: no argv for variable number of arguments in GroundedSchemaNode Python functions
Messages: 4
Date range: 9 Oct 2015 to 2015-10-09
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1504dab5dec87a9a

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 9 Oct 2015

It appears that functions for use with GroundedSchemaNodes can't use *argv for variable numbers of arguments. Variable numbers of arguments would be quite handy for creating more general purpose functions.

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-09

If its any consolation, it doesn't work in scheme either. I'm overwhelmed and don't have time to fix. Open a bug report? Can you hack around it?

--linas

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 2015-10-09

It's not currently a road block for me. It does though kind of cramp my thinking regarding uses for GSNs in my architecture.

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-09

Jim, if you are coming up with 'generic' GSNs, describe them, and maybe they could be hard-coded as atoms.

My current philosophy is that its OK to create atoms for any function or behavior that is 'simple' and can be 'well-defined'. Some recent additions include StateLink, DefineLink, DefinedSchemaNode, DefinedPredicateNode.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Old PLN GitHub issues (14ca1eec6da0a667)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Old PLN GitHub issues
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-04-09 to 2015-04-18
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ca1eec6da0a667

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-04-09

Hi,

I just closed several old PLN GitHub issues that referred specifically to implementation issues in the deprecated version.

However, the following issues are more related to syntax or conceptual issues, and as such, they may contain valuable information that could be applied to the new implementation.

At some point, perhaps someone familiar with the rule engine implementation could review these issues and take note of any valuable information, and then close them?

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2015-04-18

HI,

I have created a milestone to follow up on these and other related issues involving deprecating the python PLN.
https://github.com/opencog/opencog/milestones/Fully%20Port%20to%20URE%20based%20PLN
